---
title: "IMNCI"
subtitle: "Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness"
author: "Dr. Arun Mitra<br>Department of Community Medicine"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    logo: logo.png
    css: style.css
    embed-resources: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    center: false
    hash: true
    footer: "IMNCI | Community Medicine"
---

## Session Roadmap

-   What and Why of IMNCI?
-   The IMNCI approach: age groups and colour-coded triage
-   Describe the Advantages of applying IMNCI
-   Describe the principles of Integrated Care
-   Assess and Classify the sick child
-   IMNCI in India: delivery and F-IMNCI

## A Triage Scenario

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
You are the only health worker at a sub-centre. Three children arrive at once:

-   a 3-year-old with a **cough**
-   an 8-month-old who is **drowsy and refusing to feed**
-   a 2-year-old with a **runny nose**

You cannot see all three at once. **Who do you attend to first?**
:::

::: notes
Students will pick the drowsy, not-feeding infant. Name what they just did: they triaged by severity using a danger sign. IMNCI makes that instinct systematic and usable by a non-specialist. \~3 min.
:::

## Define IMNCI

> IMNCI is a way of case detection and treatmentusing simple clinical signs/symptoms and empiricaltreatments among children under 5 years.

<br>

> It is a case management process for a first-level facility such as a clinic, a health center or an outpatient department of a hospital

## What Is IMNCI?

A standardised, symptom-based (syndromic) approach to assessing, classifying, and managing sick children from **birth to 5 years** at the primary-care level.

-   "Integrated": a single child is assessed for **all** the major killers in one visit
-   Designed for a frontline worker, without a diagnosis-per-disease or laboratory back-up

## Why an Integrated Approach?

-   Most sick children present with signs and symptoms related to more than one condition.

-   Single diagnosis - not possible or appropriate. Treatment complicated by the need to combine therapies

-   Many are not properly assessed & treated, parents are poorly advised.

-   Providing quality care to sick children is a serious challenge.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The malnutrition–infection vicious cycle is the best justification for integration: you cannot treat the pneumonia and ignore the wasting.
:::

## Evolution of IMNCI

-   To end preventable child mortality, WHO-UNICEF designed the Integrated Management of Childhood Illness strategy in the mid 1990s

-   Launched in countries which had \>40 U-5 deaths/1000 live births

-   Laboratory and radiology services are limited or non-existent in primary health care facilities

-   To improve access & quality of care for newborns and children in primary health care services

## Problem Statement

-   In 2020, 5 million U-5 deaths, mostly from preventable and treatable causes.

-   Half among newborns

-   Half of all U-5 deaths in occurred in 5 countries: Nigeria, India, Pakistan, Congo and Ethiopia.

-   Nigeria and India alone account for 1/3rd of all deaths.

## SDG Goals

> Goal 3.2, by 2030:

> end preventable deaths of newborn and children under 5 years of age, with: - all countries aiming to reduce neonatal mortality to at least as low as 12 per 1000 live births and  - under-5 mortality to at least as low as 25 per 1000 live births.

## From IMCI to IMNCI {.smaller}

|   | IMCI (WHO/UNICEF, mid-1990s) | IMNCI (India) |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Age range | 1 week – 5 years | **Birth – 5 years** |
| Emphasis | Childhood illness (2 mo–5 y) | **Adds a strong newborn/young-infant arm** |
| Why | — | A large share of India's under-5 deaths are **neonatal** → the "N" |

## Three Components of the Strategy

1.  Improve the case-management skills of health workers (training, chart booklet)
2.  Improve the health system that delivers care (drugs, supplies, referral, supervision)
3.  Improve family and community practices (care-seeking, feeding, immunisation)

::: {.callout-tip .fragment appearance="simple"}
Two of the three pillars are **systems and community**, which is why IMNCI sits in Community Medicine, not only Paediatrics.
:::

# The IMNCI Approach

## Two Age Groups, Two Forms

| Age band | Name | Key feature |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Birth up to 2 months | **Sick young infant** | Subtle signs, rapid deterioration, low threshold to refer |
| 2 months up to 5 years | **Sick child** | General danger signs + four main symptoms |

Each band has its **own** algorithm and case-recording form.

## The Colour-Coded Classification

| Colour | Meaning | Action |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| <span style="background-color: pink; font-weight: bold;">PINK</span> | Severe classification | Urgent **pre-referral treatment** + refer |
| <span style="background-color: yellow; font-weight: bold;">YELLOW</span> | Needs specific treatment | Treat at facility / home |
| <span style="background-color: green; font-weight: bold;">GREEN</span> | Mild / no severe sign | Home management + counsel |



## Advantages of IMNCI Approach

-   Early recognition of serious conditions and prompt referral

-   Speeds up the urgent treatment and treatment seeking practices

-   Avoids wastage of resources by using most appropriate medicines and treatment

-   Reduces duplication of efforts

## The Case-Management Process

A fixed sequence for every sick child:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Assess] --> B[Classify] --> C[Identify Rx] --> D[Treat] --> E[Counsel] --> F[Follow-up]
```

Classify uses the colour code; the colour tells you what to do next.

::: notes
Walk left to right once. Emphasise it is the same sequence every time, which is what makes it reproducible. \~2 min.
:::

## Steps

![](images/clipboard-3758948906.png)

## Steps

![](images/clipboard-2231509465.png)

## Step 1: General Danger Signs (check first)

Check **before** anything else. Any **one** means the child is severely ill:

1.  Not able to drink or breastfeed
2.  Vomits everything
3.  Convulsions
4.  Lethargic or unconscious

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Any one danger sign → urgent referral.** The "any one" rule, just like the SAM criteria.
:::

::: notes
Danger signs are always assessed first. Repeat: any one is enough. \~2 min.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Four Main Symptoms {.smaller}

| Main symptom | What you look for | Severe (PINK) example |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Cough / difficult breathing** | Fast breathing, chest indrawing, stridor | Chest indrawing → severe pneumonia |
| **Diarrhoea** | Dehydration, duration, blood | Severe dehydration; dysentery |
| **Fever** | Cause, duration, measles, stiff neck | Very severe febrile disease |
| **Ear problem** | Pain, discharge, swelling behind ear | Mastoiditis |

::: notes
Overview table; the next slides drill the two highest-yield ones. \~2 min.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Cough or Difficult Breathing

-   Assess for fast breathing, chest indrawing, and stridor
-   Chest indrawing or stridor → **severe pneumonia (PINK)**

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Fast breathing thresholds (examinable):**

-   2 months up to 12 months → **≥ 50 breaths/min**
-   12 months up to 5 years → **≥ 40 breaths/min**
:::

## Diarrhoea

-   Classify dehydration: **severe / some / none** (skin pinch, sunken eyes, ability to drink)
-   Also identify **persistent diarrhoea** (≥14 days) and **dysentery** (blood in stool)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**\[→ Malnutrition L2\]** Zinc plus low-osmolarity ORS for 14 days shortens childhood diarrhoea.
:::

## Fever & Ear Problem

-   **Fever:** assess cause and duration; look for measles and for stiff neck (very severe febrile disease / meningitis)
-   **Ear problem:** ear pain or discharge; a tender swelling behind the ear suggests **mastoiditis**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Then Check: Malnutrition, Anaemia, Immunisation

-   **Acute malnutrition:** oedema of both feet, **MUAC (6–59 months)**, weight-for-height, visible severe wasting
-   **Anaemia:** **palmar pallor** (severe vs some)
-   **Immunisation status**, vitamin A, and any other problems

::: notes
Every sick child is also screened for malnutrition and anaemia at the same visit. This is the integration in action. \~2 min.
:::

## Identify Treatment, Treat, Counsel, Follow Up

-   **Treat:** for PINK children, give **pre-referral treatment** first — first antibiotic dose, prevent low blood sugar, keep warm, treat convulsions
-   **Counsel:** feeding and fluids during illness, and the **danger signs that mean "return immediately"**
-   **Follow-up:** a scheduled return visit to reassess and reclassify

## Why a Separate Algorithm

-   Signs in a young infant are **non-specific** and deterioration is **rapid**
-   So the threshold to refer is deliberately **low**
-   The three priorities: **infection, jaundice, feeding**

## PSBI / Very Severe Disease

Any **one** of the following → **PINK → refer** after pre-referral treatment:

-   Not feeding well
-   Convulsions
-   **Fast breathing (≥ 60 breaths/min)**
-   Severe chest indrawing
-   Fever (≥ 37.5°C) **or** low body temperature (\< 35.5°C)
-   Movement only when stimulated, or no movement at all

## Jaundice, Feeding & Low Weight

-   **Jaundice:** severe jaundice → refer
-   **Feeding problem or low weight for age:** assess the breastfeed — attachment, positioning, effective suckling

## Who Delivers IMNCI

-   Introduced under **RCH-II (2005)**, now implemented within the **National Health Mission**
-   Delivered by a trained team: **Medical Officers, ANMs, Anganwadi Workers, ASHAs**
-   **Home-Based Newborn Care (HBNC)** by ASHAs complements the young-infant arm in the community

::: notes
Each cadre works within its role; the strength is the trained team, not any one worker. \~2 min.
:::

## F-IMNCI (Facility-Based)

-   Trains **Medical Officers and staff nurses** at CHC / FRU / district hospital
-   To manage the sick young infants and children **referred** by outpatient IMNCI
-   Bridges the community algorithm and **inpatient** care

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**\[→ Malnutrition L2\]** F-IMNCI at the facility and the **NRC** for complicated SAM are the two arms of the same referral level, both under POSHAN 2.0 / child survival.
:::

## Algorithm

![](images/clipboard-334960444.png)

## Algorithm

![](images/clipboard-2328860758.png)

## Case Study {.smaller}

> An **11-month-old girl**, cough for 3 days. On assessment: **drinks eagerly**, respiratory rate **54/min**, **no chest indrawing**, no stridor. **MUAC 11.2 cm**, no oedema, appetite present, immunisation up to date.

::: fragment
-   Does she have any general danger sign?
-   Classify her breathing. What colour?
-   Classify her nutritional status. What colour, and what pathway?
-   What treatment and counselling, and when should she return?
:::

## Case: Working Through It {.smaller}

-   **Danger sign?** None (drinks, no vomiting, no convulsions, alert)
-   **Breathing:** RR 54 at 11 months is fast (≥ 50), no indrawing/stridor → **Pneumonia (YELLOW)**: oral amoxicillin, return in 3 days
-   **Nutrition:** MUAC 11.2 cm with appetite and no oedema → **uncomplicated SAM → RUTF (CMAM)**, not a hospital referral
-   **Counsel:** feeding and fluids; return immediately if any danger sign appears

## Key Takeaways {.smaller}

-   IMNCI is **integrated and syndromic**: one child assessed for all major killers; India's version adds the **neonate**
-   **Check general danger signs first** — any one means urgent referral
-   Four main symptoms, plus **malnutrition, anaemia, immunisation**
-   **Colour decides the pathway:** PINK refer, YELLOW treat, GREEN counsel
-   IMNCI is the **front door** to the NRC / CMAM / POSHAN 2.0 system from the Malnutrition lectures

## References

-   **MoHFW (India)** — IMNCI Student Handbook and Physician Chart Booklet; F-IMNCI Participant's Manual (implemented under RCH-II / NHM).
-   **WHO / UNICEF** — Integrated Management of Childhood Illness Chart Booklet (2014); IMCI handbook.
-   Park's Textbook of Preventive and Social Medicine — RCH / IMNCI chapters (latest edition).

# Thank You
